content.full_text:
CALIFORNIA FOUNDATION
ON THE ENVIRONMENT
AND THE ECONOMY

May 9, 1991


Mr. John Wallace, Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Request for Advice on the Application
of Government Code section 89506

Dear Mr. Wallace:

This request for formal written advice pursuant to
Government Code section 83114(b) and 2 California Code of
Regulations 18329(b)¹ is submitted by the California Foundation
on the Environment and the Economy ("CFEE"). CFEE seeks guidance
from the Fair Political Practices Commission ("FPPC") on the
application of section 89506 to its activities, as specifically
requested below.

Facts

CFEE is a nonprofit charitable corporation, founded in
1979, which is exempt from taxation under Internal Revenue Code
section 501(c)(3). CFEE is governed by a 30-member volunteer
board of directors, consisting of representatives from the
business, labor, and environmental communities.

I am the current chair and a former United States
Congressman. (Only one public official serves on the board of
directors of CFEE.) Since 1984, CFEE's president has been
Patrick Mason, Ph.D., to whom the board has delegated day-to-day
managerial responsibility.

CFEE's purpose is to bring together business, labor,
community, and environmental leadership with public officials,
policy makers and expert academicians, in forums designed to
address complex economic and social issues. To achieve these
ends, CFEE generally has two or three conferences annually within
the state of California and also conducts a foreign study-travel

¹All statutory references are to the California Government
Code unless otherwise noted. All regulations are in Title 2 of
the California Code of Regulations.

909 MONTGOMERY STREET         THIRD FLOOR         SAN FRANCISCO CA 94133

415 788 1786         [SEAL]         FAX 415 986 2227


Mr. John Wallace, Counsel
May 9, 1991
Page 2


project each year.  Various public officials who are familiar
with the subject matter area of the conference or study-travel
project are invited by CFEE to participate.

     CFEE has two principal sources of funding: an endowment
from a private foundation, and contributions solicited from
businesses (principally corporations), associations, and public
agencies having an interest in the subject matter of the
particular seminar or study-travel project of CFEE. CFEE is holding.  As
an organization exempt under Internal Revenue Code section
501(c)(3), contributions to CFEE are tax-deductible.  (A list of
various past contributors is attached.)  CFEE does not employ a
lobbyist; however, some CFEE contributors are lobbyist-employers.

     CFEE's board resolutions provide that CFEE controls the
subject matter of its conferences and study-travel program,
determines where the seminars and program will take place, and
selects the public officials who will be requested to
participate.  The resolutions provide further that CFEE may not
represent to a contributor that, in exchange for a contribution,
a certain public official will be invited to or will participate
in a CFEE program.  Decisions about subject matter, location, and
invitees are made by CFEE's president, subject to ratification by
the Board of Directors.

     For the last week in September, 1991, CFEE is planning
a study-travel project to Europe, the subject of which is
environmental control technology and alternative energy.
Meetings with the Commission of the European Community will take
place in Brussels and with the United Kingdom Chapter of the
American European Community Association in London.  The
participants will be conferring with members of the European
Community parliament and with executives and staff of the
European Community Directorates for Environment, Energy, and

     2
CFEE is distinguishable from the California Legislative
Forum ("CLF") (see Bell advice, A-91-077).  According to Mr.
Bell, CLF is not exempt from federal tax under Internal Revenue
Code section 501(c)(3).  As recited in the CLF advice request,
CLF's sole function is to host a one-time annual entertainment
event (dinner) in Washington, D.C. for public officials and
others.  We also understand that, unlike CFEE, CLF is not
"ongoing in nature," one of the tests applied in construing
regulations 18239.5 and 18616(g)(3).  See Lowell advice, A-87-
212, and In re Sloan, cited therein.  Unlike the entity referred
to in Lowell, CFEE is not a "membership organization."  CFEE
receives contributions -- not dues -- as CFEE has no members.

Mr. John Wallace, Counsel
May 9, 1991
Page 3


External Relations. A delegation of California legislators and
legislative staff will be asked by CFEE to participate in this
study-travel program.

Although CFEE has made pre-arrangements with officials
of the European Community for meetings and has also pre-arranged
for travel and accommodations, CFEE has not yet received funds
from contributors who may be interested in the 1991 study-travel
program. However, CFEE has informed potential contributors of
the tentative dates, the subject matter, and that the program
will involve a delegation from the California legislature to the
European Community. CFEE has also informed potential donors
that, if there is full participation in the study-travel program,
they may send a representative to participate at their own
expense. CFEE will pay for travel, including related lodging and
subsistence, for the members of the California legislative
delegation participating in the study-travel program.

The Ethics in Government Act³

The Ethics in Government Act of 1990 imposes an annual
$250 limit on gifts to an elected or appointed state officer from
any "single source" and bans honoraria to such persons.
(Sections 89502-89505; 2 CCR section 18531.5(c).) However,
section 89506, as relevant, provides:

(a) Payments, advances, or reimbursements for
travel, including actual transportation and
related lodging and subsistence which is
reasonably related to a legislative or
governmental purpose, or to an issue or
state, national, or international public
policy, is not prohibited or limited by
this chapter if either of the following
apply:
***
(2) The travel is provided by .... a
nonprofit charitable.... organization
which is exempt from taxation under
section 501(c)(3) of the Internal


_______________________
³Chapter 9.5 of the Political Reform Act, operative January
1, 1991.

Mr. John Wallace, Counsel
May 9, 1991
Page 4


Revenue Code...4

Question

Given that CFEE is a charitable organization, tax exempt under Internal Revenue Code section 501(c)(3), are payments by CFEE for travel, including actual transportation and related lodging and subsistence, for the members or staff of the California legislature invited by CFEE to participate in the 1991 CFEE study-travel program exempt from the limitations and prohibitions of the Ethics in Government Act?

CFEE would appreciate a response to the above question within the time specified in section 83114(b), as CFEE must soon finalize plans for the September 1991 study-travel program. Should you need any additional facts in order to respond, please contact me immediately and I will be pleased to provide them to you.

Thank you for your consideration.

Very truly yours,

Paul N. McCluskey Jr.

PAUL N. "PETE" MCCLOSKEY, JR.

PM:kah


4    The exemption from limitations described in section 89506(a) applies only to payments or reimbursements for travel which are reported by the recipient on his or her annual statement of economic interest ("SEI"). (Section 89506(c).) CFEE realizes that gifts from organizations exempt from federal tax under Internal Revenue Code section 501(c)(3) are reportable by the recipient (Riddle advice, A-89-200).

CFEE would appreciate a response to the above question within the time specified in section 83114(b), as CFEE must soon finalize plans for the September 1991 study-travel program. Should you need any additional facts in order to respond, please contact me immediately and I will be pleased to provide them to you.

Thank you for your consideration.
